glossary.def: A directed graph whose nodes are operations and whose edges are tensors flowing between them; it is the data structure TensorFlow uses to represent a program. Seeing the whole graph before execution lets the runtime fuse ops, place them on devices, and serialize the model for deployment, and it is also exactly what makes backpropagation mechanical: each node records how to push a gradient from its output back to its inputs.

Two execution modes. Eager runs each operation immediately like NumPy, so tensors hold real values you can print and debug; graph mode (entered in TensorFlow via tf.function) traces the Python into a static graph the first time it runs and then reuses that fused graph, paying a one-time tracing cost in exchange for near-zero per-call Python overhead. TensorFlow 2.0 made eager the default while keeping graph mode on demand; PyTorch's torch.compile mirrors the same write-eager-compile-later pattern.

A high-level neural-network API, created by Francois Chollet in 2015, designed around progressive disclosure of complexity: the common case is one line and customization is available exactly when needed. It is TensorFlow's official high-level API (tf.keras) and, since Keras 3, runs on a choice of TensorFlow, JAX, or PyTorch backends behind one identical interface.

A layer computing y = phi(W x + b): an affine map from d_in inputs to u outputs followed by an activation. It holds a weight matrix of shape u x d_in plus a bias vector of length u, for (d_in + 1) * u trainable parameters. Keras infers d_in lazily from the first tensor that flows in, which is why a freshly built Dense layer reports zero parameters until it has seen an input shape.

Keras's three ways to define a model. Sequential is a plain list of layers for a single input-to-output chain; the Functional API expresses an arbitrary directed graph of layers, needed for multiple inputs/outputs, skip connections, or shared layers; subclassing writes an imperative call() method for models whose control flow depends on the data. Power increases left to right; most real models use the Functional API.

TensorFlow's declarative input API: a chain of transformations on a Dataset (map, shuffle, batch, prefetch, cache) that feeds the model. Its purpose is throughput, chiefly via prefetch, which lets the CPU prepare the next batch while the accelerator trains on the current one, turning a per-step cost of t_input + t_compute into max(t_input, t_compute).

The input-pipeline technique of producing batch t+1 on the CPU while the model computes batch t on the GPU, so data preparation is hidden behind computation. With balanced costs it roughly halves step time; once one side dominates the other, the win shrinks, which is why profiling decides whether you are input-bound or compute-bound. PyTorch's DataLoader achieves the same overlap via num_workers, pin_memory, and prefetch_factor.

model.fit is Keras's built-in training loop: after compile(optimizer, loss, metrics), one call iterates batches and epochs and runs the forward pass, loss, backpropagation, optimizer update, metric accumulation, and validation, while firing callbacks such as EarlyStopping and ModelCheckpoint. A fully custom loop (a tf.GradientTape plus optimizer.apply_gradients written by hand) trades that convenience for total control and is warranted only by genuinely non-standard control flow; overriding train_step is the usual middle ground.

Chollet, F. (2021). Deep Learning with Python (2nd ed.). Manning.

TensorFlow Team. tf.data: Build TensorFlow input pipelines. Official guide.
Paszke, A. et al. (2019). PyTorch: An Imperative Style, High-Performance Deep Learning Library. NeurIPS 2019.
